title: Bathroom Vanity with Built-In Step Stool
summary: Convert the dead toekick space under a vanity into a pull-out drawer that doubles as a sturdy step stool for kids — hidden when closed.
description:
The toekick recess under most bathroom vanities is empty space, and this retrofit turns it into a drawer strong enough to stand on. Pull it out, a kid steps up to the sink; push it back, the original toekick face hides it completely. It's the rare shop project that adds real daily utility to a room without changing how anything looks.

This is a modification of an existing cabinet, not a scratch build, so the exact sizes come off your vanity, not a plan. The two numbers that matter: the width of the opening left when you remove the toekick, and confirming there's at least 16" of depth behind it for the slides. If it's shallower, use shorter slides and a shallower drawer.

Because this drawer is a step, the hardware carries a person's weight. Use side-mount slides rated well over 100 lbs, not the light bottom-mount slides that come with cheap drawer kits. Preserve the toekick when you pry it off — you're going to reattach it as the drawer's face, and a matched, undamaged face is what makes the whole thing invisible.
timeLabel: 2–4 hrs depending on how the toekick was fastened

tools.note: For removing the toekick without wrecking it.
Handy to tack the toekick face on before final screwing.
Used to attach the toekick face cleanly from behind.

materials.name: Heavy-duty 16" drawer slides, side-mount
materials.note: Rated over 100 lbs — this drawer is a step, not a junk tray.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, 16" x drawer width
materials.note: The platform you stand on. 3/4" for stiffness underfoot.

materials.name: Finish nails, 2"
materials.note: To hold the toekick face for marking before final fastening.

cutList.note: Cut to the width of the toekick opening — 22-3/4" shown.
Sized to the drawer box; the surface you stand on.

steps.title: Clear the cabinet and pull the toekick
steps.body: Remove any doors from the vanity to give yourself room to work. Then carefully pry off the toekick — most are held with stapled plastic corner blocks and no glue, so they come off intact if you're patient. Keep it undamaged; it becomes the drawer face.

steps.title: Measure the opening
steps.body: Measure the width of the opening the toekick left — 22-3/4" in the example. Confirm the opening is at least 16" deep so full-length slides fit; if it's shallower, plan on shorter slides and a shallower drawer. A quick height check confirms 1x3 stock will clear.

steps.title: Build the step drawer box
steps.body: Cut a 1x3 back to the opening width and two 1x3 sides at 15-1/4". Assemble a shallow box and cap it with the 3/4" plywood platform — that thickness is what keeps it from flexing when someone stands on it. If your toekick wasn't a cut-out face, add a 1x3 front (opening width minus 1") too.

steps.title: Mount the slides
steps.body: Attach the slides per the manufacturer's directions, side-mounted, using the 100-lb-plus rated pair. Install the drawer at the tallest position the opening allows so the step sits as high as possible. If your vanity has inset drawers above, you may need to set the depth back to match.

steps.title: Reattach the toekick as the face
steps.body: Hold the old toekick over the closed drawer with a couple of 2" finish nails and mark its exact position. Pull the drawer, then fasten the face permanently from behind — a pocket-hole jig gives the cleanest result. Test the swing: closed, it should read as the original toekick; open, it's a solid step.

images.alt: Pull-out step stool drawer built into the toekick of a bathroom vanity
